aliases: Spicy Tofu Dry Noodles with Egg
Spicy Minced Pork Dry Noodles with Tofu and Egg

english: Spicy Tofu Dry Noodles with Egg
Spicy Minced Pork Dry Noodles with Tofu and Egg

food_subgroup: Dry noodle dishes with protein and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GKM (Noodle dishes) is the closest parent. No specific mazesoba or mapo noodle code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba. SNOMED CT does not cover this specific Japanese noodle preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of mapo mazesoba with egg weighs approximately 450 g, comprising boiled wheat noodles, spicy minced pork and tofu sauce, and one whole egg.

nutrient_source.title: Noodle dish with meat and tofu — composite estimation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Noodles, egg, enriched, cooked (NDB 20110); Pork, ground, 84% lean / 16% fat, cooked (NDB 10873); Tofu, firm, raw (NDB 16126); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (NDB 01129); Soy sauce (NDB 02047); Sesame oil (NDB 04058)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for mapo mazesoba. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the primary components: wheat/egg noodles (~45% by weight), spicy minced pork (~25%), firm tofu (~15%), whole egg (~10%), and sauce/condiments (~5%). Proportions are based on standard Japanese mazesoba construction. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA Foundation Foods pork and tofu entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care 2021;44(5):e1–e13. Analogous food: Noodles, instant, boiled (GI ~47, glucose reference); supported by University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mapo mazesoba. GI of 47 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles / instant noodles (GI ~47, glucose scale), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. The protein, fat, and fibre from pork, tofu, and egg are expected to lower the effective GI of the composite dish. A GI of 47 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 81.9) / 100 ≈ 38.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 18.2 × 4.5 = 81.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~2340 mg per serving) from doubanjiang, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid mapo mazesoba due to its very high sodium content (~2340 mg per 450 g serving, approaching the daily 2300 mg recommended limit in a single meal); requesting reduced-sauce or low-sodium soy sauce versions is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~38.5 per serving) from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein and fat from pork, tofu, and egg
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with mapo mazesoba due to its substantial carbohydrate load (~82 g per serving); portion control is essential, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat (~8.6 g per serving) from minced pork and egg yolk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume mapo mazesoba infrequently due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat; choosing leaner pork, reducing sauce volume, and limiting portion size are recommended risk-reduction strategies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mapo Mazesoba with Egg (麻婆まぜそば卵入り) is a Japanese dry noodle dish that fuses the flavours of Chinese mapo tofu with the mazesoba (mixed noodle) format popularised in Japan. Unlike ramen served in broth, mazesoba is a brothless preparation in which thick wheat noodles are tossed directly with a rich, spicy sauce. In the mapo variant, the sauce is built from doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), minced pork, silken or firm tofu, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and Sichuan peppercorn, producing a bold umami-forward, numbing-spicy flavour profile. A whole egg — typically a soft-boiled or raw egg — is placed on top and mixed through the noodles before eating, adding richness and creaminess. The dish is served at Ramen Matsuri in Singapore and is representative of the Japanese fusion ramen-shop genre. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g bowl provides approximately 756 kcal, 44 g protein, 29 g fat, and 82 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~2340 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 47 (low GI) based on the wheat noodle base, but the glycemic load per serving is substantial (~38.5), requiring portion awareness for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains gluten, egg, soy, and sesame allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal diets in its standard form.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as ramen?
How many calories in mapo mazesoba?
Is mapo mazesoba high in sodium?
What is the difference between mazesoba and ramen?
What allergens are in mapo mazesoba?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for mapo mazesoba. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the primary components. The GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source food (boiled wheat noodles, University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', as mapo mazesoba is a composite noodle dish and not a dairy or egg product. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA SR Legacy; no direct approved-source entry for this dish exists. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate and are therefore genuinely unknown at the composite level. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 in template default) is reported here as 2.6 g based on USDA pork and egg component data. polyunsaturated_fat_g is 1.4 g. trans_fat_g is estimated at 0.1 g from pork. 4. GI of 47 is a conservative analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle data; actual GI of the composite dish may be lower due to protein and fat content. 5. Sodium is high due to doubanjiang and soy sauce; this is a primary clinical concern. 6. The dish is not halal in standard form due to pork content. 7. All numeric fields are populated; no -1 sentinel values remain in the core macro or secondary nutrient fields. All -1 values in micronutrient fields represent nutrients not reported in the composite source data and are genuinely unknown.
